content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 13 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 13 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 13:1 — "a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal" / "a sounding bronze or a clanging cymbal" / "a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a sounding bronze or a clanging cymbal" | Literal chalkos echon — "sounding bronze" (not specifically a gong) |
| LOGOS | "a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). EMET's "sounding bronze" is more literally precise (chalkos means "bronze/brass," not necessarily a gong), but "gong" conveys the percussive resonance to modern readers. The note about the original "bronze" is preserved in EMET's commentary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 13:1 — "but have not love" / "but do not have love" / "but didn't love others"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "but do not have love" | Literal present tense |
| LOGOS | "but have not love" | Slightly more elevated register |
| PNEUMA | "but didn't love others" | Past tense; adds "others" as object |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "have not love." PNEUMA's past tense ("didn't") changes the conditional structure from present general to past, and "others" specifies an object the Greek leaves general (agape in Paul is not exclusively interpersonal — it includes love for God and love as a disposition).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:2 — "prophetic powers" / "prophecy" / "the gift of prophecy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "prophecy" | Literal propheteia |
| LOGOS | "prophetic powers" | Interprets as a capacity |
| PNEUMA | "the gift of prophecy" | Adds "gift" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "prophetic powers" (LOGOS). While EMET's "prophecy" is literal, "prophetic powers" better conveys the hypothetical scenario of possessing the full capacity. PNEUMA's "gift" adds a word not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:3 — "to boast" vs. "to be burned" — Sinaiticus textual variant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that I may boast" | Follows Sinaiticus: kauchesomaiI (boast) |
| LOGOS | "to be burned" | Follows alternative manuscript tradition: kauthesomaiI (burned) |
| PNEUMA | "I could boast about it" | Follows Sinaiticus reading, dynamic phrasing |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the Sinaiticus reading "to boast" (EMET/PNEUMA). This is a significant textual variant. Sinaiticus reads kauchesomaiI ("that I may boast"), while many later manuscripts read kauthesomaiI ("that I may be burned"). The consensus follows the primary manuscript (Sinaiticus). The "boast" reading also fits the letter's sustained critique of Corinthian boasting. LOGOS's "to be burned" follows a different manuscript tradition and, while well-attested, is not the Sinaiticus reading. The consensus phrasing follows SOPHIA's own formulation: "deliver up my body to boast."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/PNEUMA (Sinaiticus reading). LOGOS noted as following an alternative textual tradition.

---

## 13:4 — "not arrogant" / "not puffed up" / "not...proud"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it is not puffed up" | Literal physioutai — "puffed up/inflated" |
| LOGOS | "it is not arrogant" | Standard English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Love is not jealous or boastful or proud" | Combines multiple attributes; "proud" for physioutai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "arrogant" (LOGOS). EMET's "puffed up" preserves the distinctive Pauline metaphor (used throughout the letter) but is unusual in modern English. PNEUMA's "proud" is too generic — physioutai specifically means "inflated," not general pride.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The Corinthian-specific resonance of physioutai is noted in commentary.

---

## 13:5 — "does not insist on its own way" / "does not seek the [things] of itself" / "does not demand its own way"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it does not seek the [things] of itself" | Literal ou zetei ta heautes |
| LOGOS | "It does not insist on its own way" | Idiomatic English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "It does not demand its own way" | "Demand" — stronger verb |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "does not insist on its own way." PNEUMA's "demand" is slightly too strong for zetei ("seek"). EMET's literal rendering is grammatically awkward in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:5 — "resentful" / "does not reckon the evil" / "keeps no record of being wronged"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it does not reckon the evil" | Literal ou logizetai to kakon |
| LOGOS | "it is not...resentful" | Single-word rendering |
| PNEUMA | "it keeps no record of being wronged" | Vivid dynamic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "resentful." EMET's "does not reckon the evil" is a powerful literal rendering (logizetai is an accounting term — "does not keep a ledger of wrongs"). PNEUMA's "keeps no record of being wronged" captures this beautifully. However, the consensus favors the concise LOGOS rendering for consistency with the staccato style of the Greek list. PNEUMA's vivid phrasing is noted as an excellent dynamic option.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's phrasing acknowledged as particularly effective.

---

## 13:7 — "bears all things" / "bears all [things]" / "never gives up"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it bears all [things], believes all [things], hopes all [things], endures all [things]" | Literal with four panta |
| LOGOS | "Love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things" | Formal equivalent preserving fourfold structure |
| PNEUMA | "Love never gives up, never loses faith, is always hopeful, and endures through every circumstance" | Complete dynamic recasting |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The fourfold "all things" (panta) structure is a deliberate rhetorical feature that must be preserved. PNEUMA's version, while vivid and memorable, destroys the anaphoric pattern ("all things...all things...all things...all things") that gives the verse its cadence and force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The rhetorical structure is preserved.

---

## 13:8 — "Love never ends" / "The love never falls" / "love will last forever!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The love never falls" | Literal piptei — "falls" (not "fails" or "ends") |
| LOGOS | "Love never ends" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "But love will last forever!" | Dynamic; adds "forever," exclamation mark |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Love never ends" (LOGOS). EMET's "falls" is literally accurate (piptei means "falls," not "ends"), but "never falls" is unusual in English for this concept. LOGOS's "never ends" captures the permanence. PNEUMA's exclamation mark and "forever" add emotional emphasis not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's literal observation about piptei ("falls") preserved in commentary.

---

## 13:10 — "when the perfect comes" / "when the complete comes" / "when the time of perfection comes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when the complete comes" | Literal to teleion — "the complete/whole" |
| LOGOS | "when the perfect comes" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "when the time of perfection comes" | Adds "time of" and nominalizes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the perfect" (LOGOS). To teleion can mean "the complete" or "the perfect." Both are defensible. "Perfect" is the traditional rendering and conveys the eschatological fullness Paul envisions. PNEUMA's "time of perfection" adds a temporal framework not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:11 — "child" / "infant" / "child"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "When I was an infant" | Literal nepios — specifically "infant/baby" |
| LOGOS | "When I was a child" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "When I was a child" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "child" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Nepios technically means "infant" (one who cannot speak), but Paul describes speaking, thinking, and reasoning — activities of a child, not a newborn. "Child" is the contextually appropriate rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 13:12 — "dimly" / "in a riddle" / "imperfectly, like puzzling reflections"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "through a mirror in a riddle" | Literal en ainigmati — "in a riddle/enigma" |
| LOGOS | "in a mirror dimly" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "imperfectly, like puzzling reflections in a mirror" | Expanded dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "in a mirror dimly" (LOGOS). This is the traditional and widely recognized rendering. EMET's "in a riddle" preserves the OT allusion (Numbers 12:8 LXX, where God speaks to Moses "not in riddles") but is less familiar. PNEUMA's expansion adds interpretive weight.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The traditional rendering's familiarity serves the text's liturgical and devotional use.

---

## 13:13 — "abide" / "remain" / "last forever"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "now remain faith, hope, love" | Literal menei — "remain/abide" |
| LOGOS | "faith, hope, and love abide" | "Abide" — slightly more elevated |
| PNEUMA | "Three things will last forever — faith, hope, and love" | "Last forever" — dynamic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "abide" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "last forever" adds a temporal dimension ("forever") that, while theologically implied, is not stated in the text. "Abide" preserves the characteristic Pauline verb menei.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Textual variant (v.3) | 1 | Sinaiticus "boast" vs. alternative "burned" — most significant divergence |
| Rhetorical structure preservation | 2 | Fourfold "all things" (v.7), staccato virtue list (vv.4-7) |
| PNEUMA emotional amplification | 4 | "Forever," exclamation marks, expanded paraphrases |
| Traditional rendering preference | 3 | "In a mirror dimly," "the perfect," "abide" |
| Word-level theological precision | 3 | "Puffed up" vs. "arrogant," "falls" vs. "ends," "resentful" vs. "keeps no record" |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 13 is the most beloved chapter in the letter, and its translation required balancing accuracy, tradition, and poetic force. The most consequential divergence was the textual variant in v.3 (boast vs. burned), where the consensus followed Sinaiticus over LOGOS's alternative tradition. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings were often vivid and memorable (especially "keeps no record of being wronged" in v.5) but tended to amplify emotionally beyond the Greek. The consensus consistently preserved the rhetorical structures (fourfold panta, staccato list) that give the chapter its literary power, favoring LOGOS's balance of tradition and clarity.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
